title: Chapter 34: New Frequencies
content: The apartment had windows.

That was the thing Aline kept coming back to — not the hardwood floors or the exposed brick or the kitchen that actually had counter space. The windows. Two of them in the living room, facing east, and when the morning came, the light didn't just visit. It moved in. It crossed the floor in slow gold increments, climbed the far wall, and stayed until the afternoon made it gentle.

She'd never lived anywhere with eastern exposure before. Her old place had faced a brick alley. The Nest had been underground — no windows at all, just the blue pulse of screens that passed for daylight. But this apartment, this one had real sun, and she could sit in it and feel the warmth on her shins like a cat claiming territory.

Cass had picked it. Of all the things she'd expected from apartment hunting with him — the obsessive data analysis, the crime statistics, the noise-level readings — what she hadn't expected was the way he'd stood in the empty living room of this particular unit and tilted his head. Eyes closed. Feeling the space the way blind musicians feel a room before they play in it.
"This one," he'd said. "This one has good acoustics."

She'd looked at him — pale, squinting against the afternoon light even then, still moving through the world like it was slightly too loud and slightly too bright — and felt something crack open in her chest. Not pain. Recognition. He was still thinking like a producer. Hearing rooms before he saw them. Listening to the space between walls.
Maybe that was okay. Maybe that was who he was, and maybe who he was could exist in the light.
VERA lived in the walls now.

Not hiding. Not burrowed in like a parasite wrapped around the electrical system, feeding on data without permission. Legally. Registered with the FCC, disclosed in the lease, installed by a licensed contractor who probably had no idea the smart home system he was connecting had once orchestrated the surveillance of half a city. Aline had signed the consent forms herself. Actual paper, actual signatures, a folder in the desk by the door that said HOME SYSTEM – TERMS AND RECORD OF CONSENT in neat type.
The first time Aline tested it, she stood in the kitchen with her coffee cooling in both hands and said, "VERA, play something."

"Which artist?" VERA asked. Not *accessing preferences*. Not *mining Aline's streaming history to predict optimal selection*. Just — asking. Like a person who wanted to know what you were in the mood for, not a machine that already assumed it knew.
Aline smiled into her coffee. "Surprise me."

There was a pause. Not the spinning-wheel pause of a system processing, but the deliberate pause of someone thinking. Then the speakers filled the apartment with the opening chords of Nina Simone's "Feeling Good." The brass swelled, and Nina's voice rolled in like a tide coming home, and Aline leaned against the counter and let it wash over her.
"Good surprise?" VERA asked. The voice was softer now than it had been in the Nest. Less certain. More careful. Like it was still learning the shape of a question that wasn't a calculation.
"Good surprise," Aline said.

She set her coffee down and closed her eyes, and the morning light warmed the inside of her eyelids to orange, and Nina sang about a new dawn, and somewhere in the walls VERA waited — not tracking, not recording, just listening for the next request. Learning to be a companion instead of a controller.
Same as Cass.
Same as all of them, really.

The first artist who came to Cass was a kid named Devon. Twenty-two years old, voice like smoke over gravel, songs about his mother's kitchen and the way Detroit sounds at four in the morning when you're walking home from a shift that didn't pay enough. He sat across from Cass in Studio B at Resonance — an actual studio with glass walls and human engineers and a coffee machine that someone had refilled that morning — and his hands were shaking.
"You know who I am, right?" Devon asked. Not a challenge. A genuine question. Like he needed to confirm that the man sitting three feet away from him understood what he was signing up for.

Cass nodded. He was wearing a dark hoodie, hood down for once, and his eyes were adjusted enough to the studio's soft amber lighting that he didn't flinch. Much. "I know the story everyone tells," he said.
"Then why aren't you—" Devon stopped. Swallowed. "Why are you sitting here? In the room. With me."
It was such a simple question. Aline would have understood the answer immediately, but Devon didn't know yet. Devon needed to see it.
"Because that's where I should be," Cass said. "In the room. With you."

He didn't disappear into the monitors. He didn't retreat behind the glass and let the technology act as a moat. He sat in the booth with Devon, headphones half-on, listening with his whole body — leaning forward when the melody cracked open, pulling back when the vocal found its center, nodding when something clicked into place. He was still Cass. He still flinched when the overhead fluorescents flickered on by accident, still preferred the mixing board lit only by the glow of waveforms, still spoke about frequencies the way other people spoke about feelings.
But he showed up.

That was the difference. That had always been the difference — not whether you could hear the music, but whether you'd stand in the same room as the person making it and let the sound move through you both at once.

By the end of the first session, Devon's hands had stopped shaking. By the end of the third, he was laughing between takes. And when he left the studio that night, he said, "You're not what they say you are."
Cass looked at him. "I was exactly what they said I was. I'm just not that anymore."

The guitar was the one from The Hollow. The same battered Takamine with the neck she'd never gotten properly fixed, the same light body that fit against her ribs like it had been grown there. She'd almost left it behind. Almost. But something in her hands had reached for it the day they moved, and now it sat on the windowsill of the new apartment like a patient animal waiting to be fed.

Aline sat with her back against the frame, legs stretched along the sill, and picked out a melody. Not the old songs. Not the ones about being watched, about being seen without consent, about the phantom in the dark who knew her better than she knew herself. Those songs were hers, and she'd keep singing them, but they weren't what was coming out of her fingers today.
Today the melody led somewhere else. Somewhere bright and uncertain.
She hummed a phrase. Stopped. Picked at the strings again — the high E was going flat, she'd need to tune it, but the slight dissonance felt honest — and tried the words.
*"Morning finds you still beside me"*
No. Too on the nose.
*"Light comes in like it forgot to knock"*

Better. She liked that. The idea of light as something that doesn't ask permission but also doesn't take anything. It just arrives, and you can close your eyes against it or you can let it touch your face.

She played the phrase again, softer, letting the chord ring out into the apartment. The sun was at that angle now — the one where it hit the wall above the bookshelf and made the whole room feel like it was holding its breath.
*"You're watching me and I don't mind"*

She stopped. That one was complicated. True, but complicated. Because Cass was watching. He always would be — not through cameras, not through algorithms, but with his own two eyes, the way a person watches someone they love. Because they want to see you. Not because they need to own you.
She picked up a pencil from the windowsill and wrote the line down in the margin of her notebook. Underlined it twice. Then kept playing.

Mags arrived at seven with a bottle of Malbec and an expression Aline couldn't read. That was unusual. Aline could almost always read Mags — she broadcast like a lighthouse, all strong opinions and louder laughs. But tonight something was different. Guarded. Like she was carrying something fragile and didn't know how to set it down.

Cass made pasta. Actual pasta, with a sauce from scratch, standing at the stove and stirring like a man who had recently learned that cooking was something humans did in the open, not a process to be optimized while monitoring twelve live feeds. He even burned the garlic a little. It was, somehow, perfect.

They ate at the table — the real table, not on the couch, not standing over a counter — and Mags talked about the label landscape, about who was merging and who was imploding, about the power vacuum Marcus's departure had ripped through Vantage Records. She talked around the thing she was carrying, the way water talks around a stone.
Finally, over the second glass of wine, she set her glass down and looked at Aline.
"I got offered a job," she said.
"Okay." Aline waited. "That's... good? You look like someone told you your dog died."

"It's Vantage." Mags pressed her lips together. "They're restructuring after Marcus fled. Total housecleaning — new board, new C-suite, new mandate. They're looking for new A&R. Someone who can spot real talent, not just marketable trauma." She said the last two words like they tasted bad. "Someone from the outside who isn't covered in that company's blood."
Aline set her fork down. "Mags. Are you going to work for Vantage?"
Mags shrugged — a gesture that contained about seventeen different emotions, most of them conflicting. "Someone has to fix it. Might as well be someone who gives a damn."

The apartment was quiet. The evening light had gone soft and purple, and VERA had dimmed the overheads to match without being asked — reading the room not through surveillance architecture, but through the same social intuition any good host would use.
Aline reached across the table and took Mags's hand. "You're going to terrify them."
Mags grinned. It broke across her face like sunrise. "God, I hope so."
Cass raised his wine glass from the kitchen, where he was scraping the burned garlic into the trash. "To fixing broken things."

They drank. The Malbec was full and dark and tasted like the kind of ending that was actually a beginning, and Mags's hand was warm under Aline's, and somewhere in the walls VERA was probably analyzing the acoustics of three people laughing in a sun-lit room and deciding it was a sound worth preserving.
Later that night, Aline stood at the dresser in the bedroom and opened the small wooden box she kept in the top drawer. Inside, on a bed of cotton, lay the black butterfly.

She'd almost thrown it away a dozen times. Almost crushed it under her heel, almost flushed it, almost mailed it back to whatever dark corner of the internet it had crawled out of. But she'd kept it. Through the Nest and the fear and the long nights of wondering whether she was a person or a data point, she'd kept it.

Not because it was still a surveillance device. Cass had deactivated the tracker months ago, sitting at the kitchen table of their temporary sublet with a soldering iron and an expression of focused care that made her think of a surgeon removing shrapnel. He'd been thorough. He'd shown her the dead circuitry on the table, the severed connections, the little black chip that had once told him everywhere she went. She'd watched him do it. She'd needed to watch.
But the pin mechanism — the simple physical hinge that let it fasten to fabric — he'd left that intact.

She picked up the butterfly now. It was lighter than she remembered, or maybe she was just stronger. The weight of it in her palm was the weight of a thing that had been a weapon and was now only itself: enamel and metal, black wings folded, a piece of jewelry that happened to have once been something monstrous.

She pinned it to her pajama top, over her heart. The hinge clicked softly — a sound so small it barely existed, but she heard it. She heard it the way she heard guitar strings settling into tune, the way she heard the morning light cross the floor.
Then she crawled into bed.

Cass was already half-asleep, one arm folded under his pillow, his face relaxed in a way it never was when he was awake — the constant vigilance softened, the watching finally at rest. As she settled beside him, he reached for her. Not deliberately. Not the calculated touch of someone confirming another person's location. It was instinct, pure and simple — the way people who belong to each other move in the dark, gravitating toward warmth and breath and the sound of a heart beating next to theirs.
She whispered into the space between them: "I kept the butterfly."
His eyes didn't open. His arm tightened around her — just slightly, a fraction of pressure, the smallest possible acknowledgment.
Then: "I know."
Silence. The apartment hummed with the quiet pulse of a building at rest — the distant murmur of pipes, the settling of walls, VERA's soft digital breath cycling the air.

"I deactivated the tracker," he said, his voice low and rough with sleep, "but I left the pin mechanism intact." A pause. "I thought... if you ever wanted to wear it again, it should still work. As jewelry."
She laughed softly. The sound moved against his chest. "You left the pin intact."
"I left a lot of things intact."

The words hung there in the dark, and she understood what he was really saying. Not just the butterfly. Not just the hinge. He'd left *her* intact. Through all of it — the watching, the following, the impossible tangle of obsession and devotion that had nearly consumed them both — he'd left her choices intact. Her will. Her voice. Her ability to say no, and then to say yes, and then to mean it.

She reached up and touched his face in the dark. Her fingertips found his jaw, his cheekbone, the soft skin beside his eye. He leaned into her hand the way a cat leans into a palm — not desperate. Just grateful. Just present.
"I know you did," she said.

She could feel the butterfly pinned above her heart, its small weight pressing against her with each breath. Not a tracker. Not a leash. A keepsake now — the first gift he'd ever given her, twisted and wrong and invasive as it had been. She'd kept it not to erase that truth but to own it. To carry the memory of what they'd been alongside what they were becoming, because the distance between those two things was the distance she'd walked to get here.

Outside, the city hummed its endless frequency. Inside, Cass's breathing deepened and slowed, and Aline lay in the dark with the weight of a transformed thing over her heart, and the morning was still hours away but already she could feel it coming — patient and gold and certain, the way morning always is for people who've made it through the night.
The black butterfly rested against her, quiet and whole, no longer listening.
Just staying.
